items.q: How do you say 'I am going to Spain' in French?
items.why: Students confuse aller (to go - movement away from speaker) and venir (to come - movement towards speaker). 'Je vais' = I'm going; 'je viens' = I'm coming. Also: en + feminine country, au + masculine, aux + plural.

Students default to à. The rule: EN for feminine countries (most ending -e: France, Italie, Espagne); AU for masculine (au Portugal, au Canada); AUX for plural (aux États-Unis). À is for cities (à Paris).

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to visit Paris' (near future) in French?
items.why: Students add 'à' between aller and the infinitive. Futur proche structure is ALLER (conjugated) + INFINITIVE, with no preposition: 'je vais manger', 'je vais aller', 'je vais partir'.

items.q: Which auxiliary does 'se marier' take in the passé composé?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: être. “avoir” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's blended family. “nuclear family” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was the PACS introduced in France?
items.why: Exact answer: 1999. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
